description: 'karami' is claimed or recognised power to decide or act within a stated social scope. An institution may hold authority while people dispute whether it is justified. 'kirethu' (legitimate) evaluates that grounding, and 'kanuwa' (authorize) names a particular grant made under authority.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into central 'a'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before stressed central 'a'. Close the lips for 'm', carry voicing through the nose, and finish on high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The velar release of 'ka' opens into the broad tongue movement of stressed 'ra'. Final 'mi' gathers that reach behind closed lips and narrows it to a bright ending. The contour moves outward, then finds a definite centre.
usage_notes: A particular authority can be counted; authority in general is mass-like. Its holder and source come before 'karami' when they belong inside the noun phrase. Scope and duration follow the same rule, including any stated jurisdiction. When the claim needs more room, complete clauses can carry those relations. Technical control and capacity describe what a system can do; authority concerns who may decide or act in a social arrangement.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A shared system can distribute and revise authority openly instead of hiding it behind stewardship or technical necessity.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Separating authority from legitimacy and consent lets a speaker report the power claim without accepting it.

semantic_domains.community: claimed or recognised power to decide or act within a social scope
